check: A data-flow diagram exists for any feature handling sensitive data
description: Before implementation, sketch what data enters the system, where it's stored, what transforms it, and what it leaves as — even a whiteboard-level diagram, not a formal document
why_it_matters: You can't reason about spoofing/tampering/disclosure risks for data flows you haven't actually mapped out — this is the prerequisite step for everything else in this checklist

check: Every actor/system in the data flow has its identity verified appropriately
description: For each entity that sends data into the system (a user, another service, a webhook sender), confirm what mechanism proves it is who it claims to be
why_it_matters: An unverified sender identity means anything downstream that trusts 'who sent this' is trusting an assumption, not a verified fact

check: Webhook senders are verified via signature, not just source IP or a shared secret in the URL
description: Inbound webhooks (Stripe, Shopify, etc.) are verified using the provider's HMAC signature scheme, not trust-by-obscurity (a hard-to-guess URL) or IP allowlisting alone
why_it_matters: IP allowlists can be spoofed or become stale, and a URL is not a secret once logged anywhere (browser history, proxy logs, referrer headers) — signature verification is the only reliable authenticity check

check: Data integrity verified for anything that crosses an untrusted boundary
description: Data received from the client (form submissions, API payloads) or from a third party is validated for both shape (schema) and business-rule integrity (e.g. a price the client claims can't simply be trusted) before use
why_it_matters: A client can send any value it wants regardless of what the UI allows — server-side integrity checks are the only real trust boundary, not client-side validation

check: Client-supplied prices/quantities/totals are never trusted for payment processing
description: Payment amounts are computed/verified server-side from the actual order contents in the database, never taken directly from a client-supplied total field
why_it_matters: A tampered client-side price is one of the most direct ways an attacker can under-pay for goods/services if the server trusts a client-supplied amount

check: Audit trail exists for modifications to sensitive records
description: Changes to sensitive data (user roles, payment records, admin settings) are logged with who changed what and when, ideally in an append-only fashion
why_it_matters: Without a tamper-evident audit trail, detecting and investigating unauthorized modification after the fact is far harder — this pairs with SEC026/SEC041's logging requirements

check: Actions with real-world consequence are attributable to a specific authenticated principal
description: Money movements, data deletions, and privilege changes are logged against the specific user/service account that performed them, not just 'the system did X'
why_it_matters: Without clear attribution, a disputed action (a user claiming 'I didn't do that') can't be resolved, and internal misuse is harder to detect and hold accountable

check: Logs are protected from post-hoc modification by the actors they record
description: Application logs and audit trails are written to a system the logged actor (including an admin) can't retroactively edit or delete from their own access level
why_it_matters: A tamperable audit log doesn't actually solve repudiation — an insider threat with access to modify logs can rewrite the record of their own actions

check: Data exposure mapped explicitly per API response, not assumed minimal
description: For each endpoint, explicitly list what fields it returns and confirm none are unintentionally over-exposed (e.g. an internal cost field alongside a public price field)
why_it_matters: API responses often over-fetch and return more than the frontend actually uses, silently exposing internal data to anyone who inspects network traffic — this needs an explicit check, not an assumption

check: Error messages reviewed for what they reveal about system internals
description: Beyond the general 'no stack traces in production' rule (SEC016), review specific error messages for information leakage — e.g. 'email not found' vs 'invalid credentials' on a login form reveals whether an email is registered
why_it_matters: Small information leaks (user enumeration via differing error messages) compound into meaningful reconnaissance value for an attacker planning a targeted attack

check: Resource-intensive operations are rate-limited or queued, not directly exposed
description: Any endpoint triggering expensive work (report generation, bulk export, complex search) is rate-limited per user and/or moved to an async job (BE051) rather than processed synchronously on every request
why_it_matters: An unprotected expensive endpoint is a trivial denial-of-service vector — a handful of concurrent requests can exhaust server resources for all users

check: No unbounded loops or recursive operations driven by user input
description: Pagination, recursive data structures (nested comments, category trees), and batch operations have an enforced maximum depth/size driven by server-side limits, not just client-side assumptions
why_it_matters: User-controlled unbounded operations (a maliciously deep comment thread, an unbounded batch size) can degrade or crash the service even without deliberate high-volume traffic

check: Role/permission checks happen server-side at the point of action, not derived from a client-supplied role claim alone
description: The server independently verifies the acting user's actual current role/permissions from its own source of truth at the moment of the action, rather than trusting a role value the client sent or a stale cached claim
why_it_matters: A client-supplied or stale role claim can be tampered with or simply out of date — the server's own authoritative check at action time is the only reliable enforcement point

check: Mass assignment / over-posting prevented on update endpoints
description: Update endpoints only accept and apply the specific fields intended to be user-editable (an explicit allowlist), not every field present in the request body (e.g. a user updating their profile can't also set their own isAdmin field)
why_it_matters: Mass assignment vulnerabilities let an attacker set fields they were never intended to control simply by including them in a request body that the server naively applies wholesale

check: Trust boundaries explicitly identified between frontend, backend, third parties, and infrastructure
description: The team can articulate, for a given feature, exactly where 'trusted' data becomes 'untrusted' as it crosses each of these boundaries — not just implicitly assume the whole system is one trust zone
why_it_matters: Every threat above depends on correctly identifying where trust boundaries actually are — a feature reviewed without this mapping tends to miss the specific boundary where a real vulnerability lives

check: Internal service-to-service calls are still authenticated, not implicitly trusted by network location
description: Services calling each other inside the same VPC/network are still authenticated (mTLS, service tokens, or similar) — 'it's on the internal network' is not treated as sufficient authorization on its own
why_it_matters: Network-location-based trust breaks down the moment any part of the internal network is compromised (a vulnerable dependency, a misconfigured service) — an attacker who lands inside the perimeter shouldn't get free rein

check: Third-party libraries with code-execution capability reviewed before adoption
description: A new dependency, especially one with broad permissions or that executes arbitrary code (build plugins, CLI tools run in CI), is reviewed for maintainer trustworthiness and scope before being added
why_it_matters: A compromised or malicious dependency has the same execution context as your own code — supply-chain attacks via popular package registries are an established, growing attack vector

check: Third-party SDKs embedded client-side are scoped to minimum necessary data access
description: Analytics, chat widgets, and other client-embedded third-party scripts are configured to collect/access only what's actually needed, and their network requests are periodically reviewed
why_it_matters: A client-embedded third-party script runs with the same DOM/data access as your own frontend code — overly broad third-party access is a data-exposure risk independent of any vulnerability in your own code

check: Threats prioritized by realistic likelihood × impact, not treated uniformly
description: After identifying threats via the categories above, rank them by actual likelihood and business impact rather than treating every theoretical threat as equally urgent to address
why_it_matters: Not every identified threat deserves the same remediation urgency — a threat modeling exercise that produces an unprioritized wall of findings tends to result in nothing getting fixed rather than the highest-risk items getting fixed first

check: Threat model revisited when the feature's data flow changes materially
description: A threat model isn't a one-time document — when a feature's data flow changes significantly (a new third-party integration, a new user role, a new data type collected), the threat model is revisited
why_it_matters: A stale threat model that no longer reflects the actual system gives false confidence — the discipline of revisiting it is what keeps it useful over the feature's lifetime
